evidence_quotes.quote: Let's say you get a Netflix subscription.
This is a service, which can be shut down anytime, and this is fair, as you can't expect the company to provide this servie to the end of time.

Video games have taken a middle road, where they will provide a game as a single purchase, but will still require an internet connection (to their own servers) to be played. They will also add some clauses, where they can stop that service anytime, and thus render your game unplayable.
This is an issue created by the developers, where they intruduce "optional" features, but the game can't function without them, even if you don't actually need to spend extra money.
If a game has a one time purchase price, then a minimum time of service should be enforced, and if that fails, refunds (full or partial) would be appropriate.

paragraphs.text: In the last 10-15 years, video game companies have tried to walk fine line between selling a product and selling a service, while reaping the benefits of both practices. This initiative tries to bring some light to the issues https://eci.ec.europa.eu/045/public As a lot of people don't play video games, I will give some examples using Movies / TV shows as an analogy. Let's say you get a Netflix subscription. This is a service, which can be shut down anytime, and this is fair, as you can't expect the company to provide this servie to the end of time. You don't actually own those shows and movies, and the price is relatively modest. Now let's think about buying a DVD movie. The price might be higher, and you only get one piece of media, but it's yours, and you know you will be able to watch it anytime, so long as you have a device that can play it. Video games have taken a middle road, where they will provide a game as a single purchase, but will still require an internet connection (to their own servers) to be played. They will also add some clauses, where they can stop that service anytime, and thus render your game unplayable. Now imagine if you bought a dvd movie, and it required an internet connection to Netflix for some reason. The companies will have a list of arguments on why this is needed, so let's break it down a bit: 1. "The game still receives updates to improve it, fix issues, etc." First of, updates can be downloaded separately, if the user wants them, as it was done 20 years ago when all games where purchased physically. Second, this is an issue mainly because the developers have allowed themselves to release games in broken states, full of bugs and issues, and sometimes even with incomplete content. I don't think this should be legal either, but that's another discussion. 2. "The game has online functionality, eg. in-game cash shop, and it can't work without that" This is an issue created by the developers, where they intruduce "optional" features, but the game can't function without them, even if you don't actually need to spend extra money. Additionally, when you buy the game, it is not clear what those in-game shops contain (which I don't believe should be part of any single-player games), and sometimes they add things over time as well (when or if the company decides so). 3. "The game is multiplayer, we can't be expected to provide service forever" I believe a simple solution for this would be to clarify this beforehand. If a game has a one time purchase price, then a minimum time of service should be enforced, and if that fails, refunds (full or partial) would be appropriate. It's ridiculous to pay a one time price for a game that will be shutdown in a month. If the game is advertised as a service, then an announcement should be made a certain amount of time before the service ends. Some games require both a one time payment and a service fee. I am not sure why, as one is useless without the other.
